pattern: Building a key-value map from scratch with bucket arrays and chaining demonstrates how hashing turns lookup, insert, and delete into near-constant-time operations.
hints: Spread keys across a fixed number of buckets using the remainder of the key by the bucket count.
Each bucket holds a small list of key-value pairs, since different keys can land in the same bucket.
For put, search the bucket and either update an existing pair or append a new one; for get, scan the bucket; for remove, drop the matching pair.

edge_inputs.description: statement example: put, get, update, remove, get-missing
get on an empty map returns -1
putting the same key twice updates rather than duplicates
removing a non-existent key is harmless and leaves others intact
value zero is a real stored value, distinct from the not-found sentinel
remove then re-add the same key
extreme key and value within bounds

stress.description: long randomized put/get/remove workload over a small key space so collisions, updates, and misses all occur
stress.note: small key range forces hash collisions through chaining
